Did you know that the majority of San Francisco Bay is between 10 to 15 feet deep? I didn't until I visited the Aquarium of the Bay. Located at Embarcadero and Beach Street in Pier 39, it is home to more than 24,000 marine animals and 200 species native to San Francisco Bay, including sharks, rays and river otters. Open daily from 10 to 5, the aquarium features two signature underwater tunnels stretching 300 feet in length, transparent and acrylic tunnels that immerse visitors in the vibrant marine life of San Francisco Bay. Visitors are encouraged to visit during weekday mornings to avoid peak crowd times. Established in 1996 and once named Underwater World, Aquarium of the Bay also features giant sea bass, skates, sea stars, jellyfish, brown sea nettles, moon jellies, Moray eels, bright orange Garibaldi, sturgeon, rockfish, leopard sharks, soupfin sharks, Pacific Angel sharks, anchovies and secretive octopus. Come face-to-fin with a sevengill shark, the largest predatory animal/shark in San Francisco Bay. And be entertained by the "Touch the Bay" exhibit, which allows guests to physically interact with juvenile bat rays, sea stars and other marine animals under staff supervision.
